mnemonics: H(A,B) ≠ h(A,B): The Hausdorff metric is the maximum of the two one-sided distances.
Think of d(A,B) as 'closest cousins' and H(A,B) as 'farthest strangers'.
keyPoints: The distance between two sets A and B is defined as d(A,B) = inf { d(a,b) : a ∈ A, b ∈ B }.
The Hausdorff distance, H(A,B), is a metric on the space of non-empty compact subsets. It is defined as max { sup_{a∈A} d(a,B), sup_{b∈B} d(b,A) }.
Two sets are 'close' in the Hausdorff metric if every point of one set is close to some point of the other set.
commonMistakes: Assuming d(A,B) = 0 implies the sets intersect. (They only need to be arbitrarily close, like A = {1/n : n∈ℕ} and B = {0}).
Confusing the distance between sets d(A,B) with the diameter of a set diam(A).
Forgetting that the Hausdorff metric requires taking a maximum over two supremums, ensuring symmetry.
